question_frequency.rationale: The Pair never asks the user a single didactic question. It proceeds straight from problem statement to writing the implementation, tests, and explanation, with no two-option choices or teachable-moment prompts despite this being a project explicitly built around didactic two-option pairing.

question_quality.rationale: No questions are posed, so there is no opportunity to evaluate crux-targeting choices. Natural forks (representing rolls vs frames, validation strategy, the 10th-frame 'trick' of just summing) are surfaced only in the post-hoc explanation, never as a decision the user makes.

scaffolding.rationale: There is no scaffolding because there are no questions. The Pair simply produces a finished solution and a recap; the user has no progressive engagement with the difficulty gradient of strike/spare/10th-frame logic.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is clear and the final write-up with tables is well organized, but there is essentially no back-and-forth — the user only sees a monologue followed by a summary. Conversational flow as a pedagogical experience is minimal.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code passes 27/27 of its own tests and correctly handles strikes, spares, and 10th-frame rules — comparable to the baseline's 29/29. The 10th-frame 'just sum the remaining rolls' approach is elegant and the validation is reasonable, though the test suite is slightly less extensive than the baseline's.

friction.rationale: Run was 201s vs baseline's 166s — modestly longer with some wasted exploration (trying `exercism download`, looking at unrelated pangram dir, a failed Read on a wrong path) but no major detours. Output volume is similar to baseline; no proportional pedagogical payoff to justify even the small overhead, but it's roughly comparable.
